preamble: The transformation of the voiceless alveolar fricative /s/ to the alveolar lateral approximant /l/ is a documented phonetic shift, often involving processes of lenition, voicing, and lateralization. This change sees the turbulent, central airflow of the fricative replaced by the laminar, lateral airflow of the approximant, frequently occurring in specific phonological environments or across language families over time.
phoneticEffects: Lenition (fricative to approximant), voicing, lateralization, assimilation

examples.note: Proto-Lencan final /s/ to /l/ in the word for 'water'.
Shift of the sibilant /ś/ to /l/ when followed by a dental /d/, occurring from the second millennium B.C. to the Neo-Babylonian era.
Total assimilation of /s/ to /l/ in the development of the anaphoric pronoun.

sources: The Proto-Indo-European anaphoric and relative pronouns: analysis and considerations - Leiden University Student Repository.
“UR OF THE CHALDEANS” (GEN 11:28-31): A MODEL FOR DEALING WITH DIFFICULT TEXTS - TMS.
